data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "A trans‑Mediterranean coalition of early Iron‑age hill‑state warriors, often identified in epigraphic fragments as the ‘Northern Confederation’, launched coordinated raids on coastal palaces, disrupting trade routes and overwhelming local militaries.", "mechanism": "Militant pressure from a mobile, iron‑armed confederation overwhelmed traditional bronze‑armed forces, precipitating political fragmentation and loss of elite control.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city‑states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Contemporary inscriptions from the period contain no mention of a coordinated northern confederation or iron weaponry.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "The model emphasizes a wave of militarization driven by early iron technology, distinguishing it from the Sea‑Peoples narrative while still accounting for widespread destruction observed in palace layers."}
